titleStmt.title: Masculinity, the Environment and the Phenomenology of the Body in Beowulf 1

analytic.title: Masculinity, the Environment and the Phenomenology of the Body in Beowulf 1

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: The present article advances an ecocritical and ecofeminist reading of masculinity in Beowulf. It argues that the representation of masculine embodiment and subjectivity is intricately entangled with the poem's representations of the environment, especially the bodies of water. The paper engages in a discussion with ecofeminism as well as with (eco)critical masculinity studies to elucidate the use of maritime imagery and landscape in Beowulf as a function of ideology.

div.head: Introduction: Heorot in Crisis
div.p: In Beowulf, Hrothgar's reign is presented as a continuation of Scyld Scefing's legacy. His own achievements earlier in his life have resulted not only in the further consolidation of the Scyldings' rule over the region 3 but also in the building and establishment of Heorot as the major symbol of the dynastic glory and power in the poem's world. Hrothgar's reign in the poem's narrative is, however, also riddled with a number of crises. Grendel's attack on his kingdom is a major reversal that ends the glorious part of his reign. This attack has often been read accordingly as a symbol representing human and political issues affecting the Danes as a people,4 Hrothgar's warrior-band and the ruling family.

Hrothgar's sorrow 5 sets the emotional tone for the poem's account of these crises. Even though Beowulf comes as a saviour, his ensuing success creates another crisis. Now, as Beowulf has defeated Grendel, Hrothgar offers the young hero the throne, 6 a move that might cause an internal conflict over succession in Denmark. 7 Crises also beset some other characters, for example, Unferth, and presumably many other lesser-nameless Danish warriors whose inability to handle Grendles þing (the Grendel affair) (l. 409b) has undermined both their own heroic identities and Hrothgar's worthiness as a Scylding successor.

King Hrothgar, who suffers crises of identity and self-definition, is ostensibly praised as the greatest king be saem tweonum (between two seas). 8 His retinue of Danish warriors is incapable of bringing a solution to the crisis effected by Grendel's raids. The poem is also a crisis narrative of Beowulf's self-definition as a promising prince, worthy of a kingdom. 9 That he alone is able to probe the depths of seas, fight sea-monsters and dive into Grendel's mother's mere intensifies the crisis of both succession and heroic identity at Heorot. It would appear that the tension, which I argue lies at the centre of the poem's narrative and thought, between Beowulf as an accomplished swimmer, who defeats underwater monsters and becomes throne-worthy, and Hrothgar, the king "between two seas", is never quite resolved in the poem. The maritime aesthetics of the poem's imagery may thus be probed for the role in the poem's narrative. 10 The present paper will advance an ecocritical examination of Beowulf's representation of masculinity and power and will interrogate the representation of the sea in Beowulf as well as the poem's investigation of men's minds and bodies. I argue that Beowulf represents the natural environment through malestream norms, borrowing the term malestream from Mary O'Brien's Politics of Reproduction. 11 I thus align my reading of Beowulf more broadly with ecofeminism and environmental masculinity studies. In Beowulf, the masculine body is marshalled in the poem to validate the dominant perspective from which to perceive and experience the world, creating a malestream phenomenology of masculine subjectivity as the norm of the human presence in the human and nonhuman world. The epigraph from Rosi Braidotti's The Posthuman guides the paper's argument, highlighting a distant, but provocatively resonant, kinship between the classical humanist assumption that the male and the masculine embody norms of representation and the poem's male-appropriated representation of the environment. The later sections, given to the analysis of Beowulf's sojourn at Hrothgar's Heorot, will investigate the role of water bodies in an account of the construction of heroic masculinity and masculine royal power, focusing on Beowulf and Hrothgar.

div.head: Beowulf's Malestream Phenomenal Diction: Seas and Selves
div.p: In the Old English Exeter Book, the didactic poem known under the modern editorial title Maxims I, juxtaposes the minds of rulers with the stormy sea.
Styran sceal mon strongum mode.

Storm oft holm gebringeþ, geofen in grimmum saelum; onginnað grome fundian, h fealwe on feorran to londe, hwaeþer he faeste stonde. Weallas him wiþre healdað, him biþ wind gemaene. Swa biþ sae smilte, þonne hy wind ne weceð; swa beoþ þeoda geþwaere, þonne hy geþingad habbað, gesittað him on gesundum þingum, ond þonne mid gesiþum healdaþ cene men gecynde rice.

One must steer with a strong mind. Storm often brings the sea, the ocean, into severe condition; angry, fallow waves from afar begin rushing to land, testing whether or not it will stand firm. Walls hold out against them, wind is common to both. As the sea is calm when it does not awaken its waters, so people are peaceful when they come to agreement; they sit in prosperity, and then brave men with their companions control the lawful kingdom (50-57a). 12 The passage presupposes juxtaposing the fierceness of the sea with the ordered human world. The representations of the violent sea in Beowulf might be thus viewed as a poetic device that holds up a mirror to the heroic disposition that, appraised positively as it is, can also be potentially destructive. Just as the stormy sea encroaches on land, so does anger disrupt social order. The storm and human anger are thus represented as best to exist as external to, respectively, land and society. Importantly, the sea, by way of personification, displays cognitive attributes. The verb fundian suggests inquisitiveness. The phrase hwaeþer he faeste stonde (whether or not it will stand firm) intimates that the sea constantly tries to test the firmness of land. 13 Jennifer Neville observes in her Representations of the Natural World in Old English Poetry that the environment in Old English poetry is depicted as antagonistic to humanity. Such representations, as she claims, serve to expose the fragility of the human cultural world. 14 This passage intimates yet another danger: "the natural world symbolises the forceshere human conflictcapable of destroying society". 15 The passage establishes an isomorphism between the sea and the human mind. 16 In Beowulf, too, there is an intriguing parallel between minds entangled in crisis and turbulent bodies of water. It might appear that the poem suggests that the audience imagine anxious men and the rough sea as a single isomorphic body, whose angry waves surge and subside, providing a similar pattern to human and non-human lives, history, and the environment. For example, when Hrothgar faces AEschere's death at the hands of Grendel's mother, he is on hreon mode (his mind is in distress) (l. 1307b). Earlier, when Beowulf describes his swimming match with Breca and the stormy sea, he says, hreo waeron yþa (the waves were turbulent) (l. 548b). 17 Such an isomorphism is explored by Gillian R. Overing in her essay on men Beowulf, "Men in Trouble", where she argues that in such Old English poems as Beowulf and "The Wanderer" there is "an isomorphic relation between self and world" and that "affect is understood via mind in connection with body, in dynamic relation with cultural and physical worlds". 18 In her essay, she mostly considers the double context for the use of the adjective hreo, which can refer both to mental disturbance as well as turbulent weather. 19 She suggests that in Beowulf "the storm is one of inner conflict, confusion, turmoil, or anxiety, a symptom of a self in crisis". 20 In light of the recent work done on mentality and emotion in Old English verse, it must be added, however, that in Beowulf, such representations of mentality and emotion are also pervasively aligned with the cardiocentric model of emotion as a mental swelling. 21 Beowulf comes to Hrothgar because his wish is that the king's cearwylmas colran wurðaþ (seethings of sorrow will become cold) (l. 282). Other characters in the poem experience sorhwylmas, (surges of sorrow) (l. 904b), breostwylm (surging of the breast) (Hrothgar's intense sorrow when Beowulf departs, l. 1877a). What is particularly interesting from an ecocritical perspective on affect is that this psychologising language is paralleled in the poem by references to literal storms and seas. The simplex wylm (welling, surging) (l. 516a) as well as such phrases as waeateres wylm (surging of water) (l. 1693a), flodes wylm (flood) (l. 1764), and holmwylm (surge of the sea, sea-current) (l. 2411) refer to the rough sea in Beowulf. The emotional word cearwylm (seething of sorrow) is similar in structure to such compounds as saewylm (seawelling, billow) (l. 393b) brimwylm (surge of the sea) (l. 1494). At another point, Hygelac tells Beowulf: Ic ðaes modceare sorhwylmum seað, siðe ne truwode leofes mannes (I seethed with waves of care on account of my soul's sorrow, I did not feel certain about the outcome of the dear man's journey) (l. 1992-1993). Beowulf speculates about Ingeld's violence that will arise, syððan Ingelde weallað waelniðas, ond him wiflufan aefter cearwaelmum colran weorðað (when deadly hate swells in Ingeld and his love for the wife becomes colder after the seething of his sorrow) (l. 2064-2066).

The poem's investment in the representations of entanglements of subjectivity with the world, especially through its attention to an isomorphic extension between men's embodied minds and bodies of water, suggests that its perspective on the world is phenomenological. Phenomenology elucidates the embodiment and material embeddedness of human experience, always reconstituted by the material world that pre-exists the these are wholly imported or projected from the human realm", Life-Course, 9. She prefers the term over anthropomorphism, Life-Course, 9. 17 The Toronto Dictionary of Old English lists "turbulent, fierce, cruel" as the primary meaning of hrēoh, rēow. 18 Overing, "Men in Trouble", 29. 19 Ibid.,[31][32]31. 21 Mental welling of emotional fluid is also related to what is identified as the hydraulic model of the mind discussed in Lockett, Vernacular Psychologies, 5. She provides a substantial discussion of Old English poetic vocabulary related to such mental swelling, especially verbs weallan and belgan in Vernacular Psychologies, 50.

experiencing subject. Maurice Merleau-Ponty's phenomenology, especially as developed in his Phenomenology of Perception, has already been productively applied by Daniel C. Remein's reading of Beowulf, in which he argues that the Beowulf poet develops a pervasive phenomenological poetics. 22 Merleau-Ponty presents perception and embodiment as processes that involve embodied humans and their environment as interacting partners. For instance, Merleau-Ponty's Phenomenology of Perception understands sensations as transcendental events in pre-personal and phenomenological fields. The world, for Merleau-Ponty, is not an object, but a field of experience actively constituting the human subject: "the world is inseparable from the subject, but from a subject that is nothing but a project of the world". 23 Remein suggests some important implications that this phenomenal reading has for ecocritical studies of the poem. For example, he argues that "this phenomenological tangle, as a function poem's sensological relationship to the non-human world, would precipitate a profoundly ecopoetical moment". 24 Remein's observation is potentially inspirational for understanding Beowulf as ecopoetry. 25 When approached through the lens of ecocriticism, Beowulf's phenomenological poetry is problematic, however, as such potentially ecopoetic moments are compromised by the poem's malestream representation of the environment. Firstly, Beowulf is a profoundly ideological poem in that it is pervasively invested in the production of the masculine subject as well as a complex phenomenology of the male hero's experience. 26 Secondly, the poem is more interested in establishing rather than revising the boundaries between the human and nonhuman. The poem's ideological investment in the poem's representation of landscape and seascape has been discussed by Alfred K. Siewers through the lens of ecocritical and postcolonial theory. 27 Nicole Guenther Discenza argues along similar lines that "Grendel and his mother cross from their own waste, wild space into what had been ordered, civilized space, bringing chaos and destruction into the Danes's world". 28 As Christopher Abram observes in his analysis of the fens in the poem, Beowulf "is written from and for the point of view of the 'civilized' [audience]". 29 Thirdly, especially in the episode that narrates Beowulf's combat with Grendel's mother, the poem's narrative creates a masculine subjectivity by testing it against Grendel's mother's subjectivity. The poem's account of Beowulf's attack on Grendel's mother's mere is problematic in terms of the representation of gender and race. 30 In that narrative, Beowulf emerges as a masculine 22 Remein, The Heat of Beowulf, especially 93-100. Also, David Buchanan's unpublished PhD dissertation, Phenomenal Anglo-Saxons: Perception, Adaptation, and the Poetic Imagination, uses phenomenology to elucidates the strategies of translation employed by Old English poets in the process of translating Latin literature into Old English Verse. 23 Merleau-Ponty, Phenomenology of Perception, 454. The concept of the phenomenal field is used throughout Phenomenology of Experience, especially 224-5 and 426-9. 24 Remein, The Heat of Beowulf, 92. 25 The concept of ecopoetry is defined by J.Scott Bryson in Ecopoetry: A Critical Introduction.: "Ecopoetry is a mode that, while adhering to certain conventions of traditional poetry, advances beyond that tradition and takes on distinctly contemporary problems and issues, thus becoming generally marked by three primary characteristics: an ecological and biocentric perspective reorganizing the inter dependent nature of the world; a deep humility with regard to our relationships with human and nonhuman nature; and an intense scepticism toward hyperrationality a scepticism that leads to an indictment of an overtechnologized modern world and a warning concerning the very real potential for ecological catastrophe". 5-6. Matt Low applies this definition in article on Old English elegies, "Ecopoetry", 16. 26 See Price, "Sufficient Tragedy", 682-3, for an account of secondary literature on masculinity in Beowulf. 27 Siewers, Landscapes of Conversion. 28 Discenza, Inhabited Spaces, 146. 29 Abram, "At Home in the Fens", 138. 30 See Kim, "The Question of Race"; Morrison, "Grendel and his Mother", 260-1.

self more deeply embedded in masculine heroic culture, 31 while incrementally demoting Grendel's mother from a queen, as well as a caring, vengeful, and unequivocally heroic mother, until she disappears both literally and symbolically. 32 Such representations merit a thorough discussion in light of the ecofeminist philosophy and ecocritical masculinity studies to investigate the extent to which Beowulf privileges the anthropocentric and androcentric vantage point from which the culture/ nature divide in the poem is constructed and represented. Ecofeminist thought supplies critical tools for interrogating the role of the poem's phenomenal diction in the generation of the poem's male heroic discourse. 33 For example, ecofeminists have critiqued the western notion of reason/nature binary, which results in the hyper-separation of the human from the nonhuman world as well as, in Val Plumwood's words, "the convergence of the concepts of the human and the masculine". 34 Rosi Braidotti critiques such androcentrism and anthropocentrism by critically addressing the humanist concept of the universal man. 35 In addition, ecofeminism readily perceives the world as a morethan-human and interspecies community, often pinpointing the androcentrism informing notions of human exceptionalism. For instance, the ecofeminist scholar and activist Greta Gaard discusses the androcentric character of the Anthropocene in her account of the instrumental use of animals in extra-terrestrial exploration. 36 Marti Kheel argues that masculinism "idealises transcending the biological realm, as represented by other-thanhuman animals and affiliative ties". 37 Another important field, aligned with ecofeminism, is ecological masculinities. 38 In their Ecological Masculinities, Martin Hultman and Paul M. Pulé utilise Mary O'Brien's concept of malestream norms to "highlight the ways masculinities manifest in private and public as well as personal and professional spheres, effectively normalising masculine hegemonisation". 39 Early medieval English literature has also been subject to ecofeminist criticism. 40 It is thus pertinent to question the poem's malestream diction to investigate the poem's interest in masculine subjectivity with a view to reconsidering the poem's dominant perspective and revealing non-masculine and more-than-human vantage points at the poem's 31 It is notable that while Beowulf approaches Grendel on equal footing by refusing to bear weapons to fight the opponent who refrains using a sword, he plunges into Grendel's mother's mere fully armed. In addition, only using another sword after the first weapon has failed him does he manage to kill her. In the combat with Grendel's mother Beowulf is attributed more human and fewer monstrous features. 32 Oswald,Monsters,Gender,and Sexuality,[95][96] Other important books in the field of ecofeminism include Merchant, The Death of Nature; Plumwood, Feminism and the Mastery of Nature. 34 Plumwood, Feminism and the Mastery, 25. For the concept of hyperseparation, which is also termed radical exclusion, see Plumwood, Environmental Culture, 101-3. The Western objectification of nature and women in antiquity and modernity is discussed in Merchant, The Death of Nature. 35 See, The Posthuman, 65, and, more recent, Posthuman Feminism, 68-104. 36 Gaard, Critical Ecofeminism, 92-114. 37 Kheel, Nature Ethics, 3. See also Donna J. Haraway, who introduces the concept of the Chthulucene as an alternative to the Anthropocene to counter the Western notions of human exceptionalism and integrate an interspecies perspective of the more-than-human world into her philosophical outlook. 38 See especially See Hultman and Pulé's Ecological Masculinities, where they advance the practice of caring masculinity "for the intersecting terrain between modern Western men, masculinities, and earth", 2. Their another, more recent volume, Men, Masculinity, and Earth. 39 Hultman and Pulé, Ecological Masculinities, 3. 40 See Estes, Anglo-Saxon Literary Landscapes, especially 24-25 and 147-9 for several important application of ecofeminist thought to Old English verse. There is also a recent ecofeminist reading of the Old English Wife's Lament by the indigenous scholar Andrews, "Research, Renewal". In their recent publications on Old English verse, Corinne Dale and Courtney Catherine Barajas question the assumption that medieval literature is anthropocentric.

margins. Here, I would like to create a common space for ecofeminist and critical masculinity studies to examine several episodes in Beowulf in which bodies of water serve to establish the man as subject and agent. Building from the ecofeminist premise that the masculine gaze constitutes and projects on the environment a subject/object binary, it will be argued here that the poem's representations of the sea invest military masculinity with a set of heroic roles that define men as subjects and agents. In addition, these representations forge a vital association of kingship and land, defining humanmade space as a dominion to be enclosed and protected from a hostile external other.

div.head: Beowulf's Sea-Voyage
div.p: The environment in Beowulf is a source of both aesthetic pleasure and anxiety. Both pleasure and anxiety presuppose a perspective relatable to an early English male aristocratic reader. Beowulf's travel to and arrival at Heorot represent an ecology between humans and the environment in which human-made objects are instrumental for humans to negotiate as well as relate to the environment. In her ecocritical discussion of sea-crossing in Elene, Andreas, Exodus and Beowulf, Heide Estes claims that such episodes induce "pleasure in the environment" and reveal the human confidence that the ship can defy the dangers of the waters. 41 The account of Beowulf's passage across the sea to meet Hrothgar concentrates on the ship that is described with terms that range from a travelling companion to a physical tool. Its language, most importantly, represents the ship as an agential force and as capable of being both subject and object. He told them to prepare a good wave-traverser, saying that he wanted to visit that war-king across the swan-road, that renowned prince, who was in need of men. Little did wisemen blame him for the voyage even though he was dear to them. They encouraged the valiant man and drew lots. The good leader of the Geats selected the best from among the choicest of men available to him. Fifteen of them sought the see-wood. The man led the way, an experienced sailor, towards the boundary of the region. Time went forth. The ship was on the waves, the boat under the cliff. The warriors came readily aboard the vessel. Currents mingled water with sand; the men placed bright accoutrements in the bosom of the ship, splendid armour. They moved the bound wood forward, willingly engaging in the enterprise. The foamy flier departed not unlike a bird and went on to cross the ocean of waves, pushed by the wind. Finally, by the end of the other day the ship with a bound prow advanced to a point where the sailors saw land and cliff shining, steep mountains, broad promontories. The voyage was over. The Geats readily disembarked upon the shore and moored the sea-wood (their armour and war-garment rattled) and thanked God for the safe passage across the sea (ll. 198b-228).

The pleasure created by the text lies in the representations of a potentially hostile environment as serving human purposes. I would add that the ship is an assemblage that absorbs some features of two kinds of spaces that remain external to the human world and yet are also essential to its construction and flourishing: the forest and the sea. Since wood is the material cause of the ship. Synecdochically, the ship continues to constitute part of a forest, which is a standing reserve for shipbuilding. The passage is rich in kennings and compounds that describe the ship in terms of its building material. The kenning sund-wudu (sea-wood) (l. 208a) concentrates on the material and the maritime context of its use. The compound wudu-bundene (bound wood) (l. 216b), also referring to the ship, reinforces the poem's idea of human ability to shape hard material into objects that serve human purposes. The passage might be read as an ecopoetic moment in which the sea is viewed from a nonhuman perspective. In his detailed analysis of it, Remein concludes that, as a setting, the sea "[disturbs] the representational process of the poem", 42 the process which aims at the characterisation of human heroic action. 43 Remein develops Peter Clemoes's argument that the passage in question represents a dynamic interaction between space and the human-made object. 44 Heide Estes's statement that the sea in Old English verse "is not simply a static stage for human actions, but a very strong presence, interacting with, influencing, and affecting the human characters" 45 also emphasises the ability of Old English poets to transcend anthropocentrism in their maritime poetry. Remein similarly suggests a more-than-human perspective presupposed in the passage, as he argues 42 Remein, "The Heat", 212. 43 Ibid., 208. 44 As Clemoes observes, "that this interaction is organic is borne out by the vocabulary: the sea is at first waves, then currents with depth, and finally a waegholm, a whole sea of waves; the boat changes from a utilitarian thing of specific parts, a prow and a hold, to a whole object designed to with stand the sea, wudu bundenne (a ship with a bound prow), and finally becomes a thing of pure movement, a foamy-necked floater like a bird. Both boat and sea undergo development within their distinctive beings". See: Clemoes, "Action in Beowulf", 152. 45 Estes, Anglo-Saxon Literary Landscapes, 35-36. that it is characterised by the poem's "non-representational horizon and an ecopoetical trajectory". 46 I would suggest, however, that the narrative closeness to the account of the horrors besetting Heorot heightens an androcentric perspective that pervades the passage. Estes's statement that such representations offer pleasure to the reader is thus insightful. Insofar as it provides a relief from the horrors that the Danes are experiencing at Heorot, the passage is reassuring for the reader. In addition, Clemoes points out that the voyage "is a qualitative development by which the uncomplicated action of the ship is replaced by, issues in, makes directly possible, another, fuller, more significant action by the men". 47 The sea-crossing, like the building of Heorot, is therefore a context for a construction of masculine authority. As Hrothgar ordered Heorot to be built (hatan wolde, l. 68b), Beowulf ordered his men (het him, l. 198b) to prepare the ship for the voyage. It is thus notable that the passage intimates that Hrothgar lacks men, him waes manna þearf (Hrothgar was in need of men) (l. 201b). As human-made objects, Beowulf's ship and Hrothgar's Heorot diverge in enhancing contrasting emotions of pleasure and anxiety. Beowulf's ship illustrates the success of human attempts to command the environment.

div.head: The Sea in the Breca Episode
div.p: Heorot has a complicated relationship with the wilderness of the moors inhabited by Grendel and his mother. 48 Rather than a place productive of pleasure, with a view to counterbalancing the pleasure derived from Beowulf's safe sea-crossing, Heorot becomes a place of violent death inflicted on male bodies, an object that generates masculine anxiety over heroic identity. When Beowulf arrives at Heorot, Hrothgar himself gives a poignant account of his men's failed attempts to defeat Grendel after they ritually promised victory over a ceremonial mead-cup. This point validates the poet's intimation, parenthetically articulated during the account of Beowulf's sea-crossing, that him waes manna þearf (Hrothgar was in need of men) (l. 201b). Drunken with beer, warriors often vowed over cups of ale that they would brace themselves for Grendel's violence and await him with edges of their swords in the beer-hall. Then that 46 Remein, The Heat of Beowulf, 200. 47 Clemoes, "Action in Beowulf", 158. 48 As Christopher Abram argues, for example, the intention to demarcate the humanmade space against the fens is challenged internally within the poem. He reads Heorot as "something alien, something out of place" and points out that "Heorot is, presumably, built on the edge of the fen" and that "the two locations [that is, Heorot and Grendel's mere] are part of the same landscape". See: Abram, "At home in the Fens", 133-4.

noble mead-hall was blood-stained in the morning. When the light of day appeared, all bench-plans were wet with battle-blood, the hall reddened with swords; I had fewer dear men in my loyal retinue when death took them. Now take your seat and take part in the feast and disclose your counsel, victorious glory to men as you mind dictates (ll. 480-490).

As Hugh Magennis argues, in Old English verse "feasting expresses metonymically the theme of life in the hall and in doing so it provides one of the most familiar and suggestive bodies of imagery". 49 In this passage, the connection between drinking and life is turned upside-down, however. In bitterly ironic terms, Grendel's decimation and dismemberment of Hrothgar's retinue represents a horrific reversal of consumption as social practice. The use b-alliteration conflates the imagery of ceremonial bear-drinking with bloodshed (bencþelu blode, l. 846). There are also words that sustain a graphic image of Heorot as stained with blood and gore: driht-sele dreor-fah (the hall was stained with gore) (l. 485a), blode bestymed (wet with blood) (l. 486b), heall heorudreore (the hall reddened with swords) (l. 487a). The passage artfully exposes the instability of the symbolic mode that underlies the poem's representation of heroic culture. While in Old English verse the cup is a symbol of normative and human consumption, Hrothgar's description conflates drink and bodily fluids.

The anxiety that informs Hrothgar's description of the present state of affairs at Heorot immediately finds another expression in Unferth's questioning of Beowulf's heroic prowess when he tries to shame the hero by claiming that he once lost a swimming contest. Not only does he claim that Beowulf lost the match, but also that Beowulf was, in the first place, reckless to participate in it. 50 Unferth is an important voice at Heorot. The role of þyle, which is attributed to Unferth in Hrothgar's court, is difficult to define precisely and might range from the counsellor and spokesman to jester. 51 This difficulty is coupled by the ambiguous appraisal of Unferth's heroism (as the poet attributes to Unferth jealousy of Beowulf's heroism 52 and points to his cowardly unwillingness to fight Grendel 53 ) as well as his role in the history of Hrothgar's royal house (the killing of his brothers 54 and the intimation that he would be involved in the future conflict between Hrothulf and Hrothgar's sons). 55 It has nonetheless been suggested that the position of þyle was rather elevated. Carol J. Clover claims that one of the duties of þyle might include "the hostile investigation into the reputation of a newcomer". 56 Michael J. Enright draws upon Old Norse literature, in which "the þulr was a kind of orator with 49 Magennis, Images of Community, 43. 50 (ll. 508-510a). 51 Unferth is referred to as þyle twice in the poem: at lines 1165 and 1456. Editors of Klaeber's Beowulf, IV edition, gloss it as "orator, spokesman, official entertainer", Klaeber's Beowulf, 448. The Dictionary of Bosworth and Toller translates the word as "an orator, spokesman". The idea that the word means a jester was developed by Eliason, "The þyle and the Scop"; Robinson, "Elements of the Marvellous". 52 Unferth's jealousy can be construed in different ways. While Robinson, "Elements of the Marvelous", 86-91. 129, considers him to be an unheroic and Falstaffian figure, it has also been suggested by critics who view Unferth as an established member of Hrothgar's comitatus that the feeling of jealousy comes from Unferth's fear that his own heroic deeds might be diminished by Beowulf's success. See Neidorf, "Unferth's Ambiguity", especially 451-3; Neidorf, "On Beowulf and the Nibelungslied", 664, for such a positive appraisal of Unferth. Unferth's jealousy is also addressed in Price, "Sufficient Tragedy", 685-9. 53 Beowulf points out that Unferth has never dared to fight Grendel. The poet also states later that Unferth did not dare to fight Grendel's mother (lines 1468b-1471a). 54 Beowulf refers to Unferth's fratricide (lines 587-9). The claim is validated later by the narrator (lines 1167b-1168). 55 Lines 1162-8 suggest such an interpretation of future events at Heorot. See Fulk (et al.), Beowulf, 117, for a critical account of such interpretations. 56 Clover, "The Germanic Context", 146.

a special seat in the hall and connection to a king", and further argues for the important place that Unferth occupies in Hrothgar's warband. 57 It can be surmised that it is expected of Unferth to defend the honour of the Danes and question any newcomer warrior whose heroism might diminish the manhood of the Danish warriors. 58 In both Unferth's insulting speech and Beowulf's reply, the sea is important as a setting for masculine action as well as a symbolic referent for an examination of heroic masculinity. As a þyle, Unferth addresses the newcomer Beowulf as a rival for the comitatus of the Danish warriors. He evokes the maritime setting of the match to describe a possible deficiency in Beowulf's character.

Git for wlence wada cunnedon ond for dolgilpe on deop waeter aldrum neþdon you tried the deep waters for pride and out of foolishness, risking your lives (ll. 508-510a) To illustrate this deficiency, Unferth frames the famed story of Beowulf and Breca's swimming match in a contest of natural elements: geofon yþum weol, /wintrys wylmum (the ocean seethed with waves, the surging of winter) (ll. 515b-516a). His words suggest an isomorphism of the sea and human affects. Unferth's framing of such an isomorphism, however, implies a censure of the hero's desire to assert his masculine strength over elements and over another man as reckless. Unferth thus uses the sea to imply that Beowulf failed to endorse a truly heroic ideal of masculinity that combines the strength of the body with the strength of the mind.

Beowulf's version seems to reinvent his opponent's account not only by endowing the sea with more life and agency but also by interlacing human and nonhuman agencies. We had been together in the sea for five nights until the current, welling waves, the coldest weather, dark night, and the battle-grim north wind set as apart; the waves were angry, the minds of the sea-fish were disturbed (ll. 544-549).

When Beowulf fights against sea-monsters in his account of his swimming match with Breca, the waves are hreo (ferocious, angry) (l. 548b), a word that has a human referent elsewhere in the poem (l. 1307b). The anger of the sea reflects the hostility of sea-beasts: waes mere-fixa mod onhrered (the courage, literally, also minds, of the fish was whetted) (l. 549). Both accounts characterise the sea and sea creatures as driven by affects mirroring human emotions and mentalities. However, before Beowulf gives his own version, he insists that his merestrengo (sea-strength) (533a) was greater than any other man's. 57 Enright, "The Warband Context", 299-300. 58 As Basil Arnould Price observes in his study of masculinity in Beowulf, "Heorot, the hall of Hroðgar, is a homosocial context that provides a setting for men to secure their masculinity by competing with and dominating one another in social, martial, and economic competitions". See: Price, "Sufficient Tragedy", 685.

Beowulf's maritime poetics are thus framed by his desire to assert his superior status as man and warrior. 59 Unferth the þyle implies that wisdom is the chief component of a heroic mental frame. Beowulf counters him by driving home the reality of physical perils circumscribing the courtly values which Unferth implies to lie at the heart of the heroic ideal. Beowulf's evocation of the sea's ferocity thus emphasises the need for the hero to embrace and control a corresponding ferocity within himself as an ideal of heroic self-mastery.

In addition, Beowulf adduces his sea-adventure to mediate the present crisis at Heorot through the use of humour. He says, for example, that he served the sea-monsters with his precious sword. Beowulf gives some force to the symbolism enacted by the social practice taking place at the feast. The foes frequently threatened me severely. I served them a costly sword, as was fit. The malefactors did not take pleasure in the feast, as they failed to make a meal of me at their table at the bottom of the sea; but in the morning, wounded with the sword, on the shore they lay put to sleep with the heirloom, so they could not obstruct sailors on the deep water-way (ll. 559-569a).

Beowulf's humorous use of fylle (feast) (l. 562) alludes to Grendel's "waelfylle" (abundance of the slain) (l. 125a) of the bodies of Hrothgar's men that he carried from Heorot. His statement that he served the sea-monsters (þenode, l. 560b) may humorously allude to the male attendant serving drinks earlier (ll. 491-498). His grim joke offers an account of a combat in which human objects can successfully operate within the symbolic and ideological mode of the language of feasting and bear-drinking. The bed of the sea is described by Beowulf as a parody of the hall-floor on which retainers sit around and enjoy a banquet. The beasts ended up being killed by Beowulf, and in the morning their corpses lay asleep on the seashore, an image that is also a humorous reversal of the Danes asleep after a feast at the moment of Grendel's first attack: the Danes swefan aefter symble (slumbered after the feast) (l. 119a). Beowulf's reply to Unferth's investigation is an act of translation that constructs a maritime setting for masculine endeavour. The sea that Unferth represents in his version of the account becomes, in Beowulf's handling, a space for masculine pursuit and a textual object for the ironic representation of the sea of trouble that besets the Danish men and their ruler. Beowulf handles Unferth's isomorphic reference, aimed at exposing his recklessness, with a view to bringing up a forceful articulation of the crisis at Heorot. He also articulates the crisis as resulting from the lack of courage and heroism on the part of the Danes.

div.head: Armoured Phenomenology and Grendel's Mother's Mere
div.p: As Nicole Guenther Discenza observes, Beowulf's cleansing of Heorot from Grendel restores the hall to its proper role. 60 However, Beowulf's defeat of Grendel displays a potential for another crisis. When Grendel, defeated and armless, enters the mere, its waters become another body of turbulent water in a provocative proximity to another crisis that is to beset the Danes: ðaer waes on blode brim weallende, atol yða geswing eal gemenged haton heolfre, heorodreore weol
The water was seething and boiling with blood, the menacing rolling of waves, filled with hot gore, swelling with battle-dread (ll. 847-849).

The passage uses some of the vocabulary that Leslie Lockett finds typical of how the cardiocentric heat is evoked to characterise psychological distress in Old English verse. 61 While weallande and weol conventionally appear in descriptions of seething and swelling angry minds, they here indicate the turbulence of the mere's waters. Grendel, when approaching Heorot, is described as gebolgen (angry) (723b), and the verb belgan as well as the past participle gebolgen are, as Lockett indicates, strongly associated with such minds seething with the heat of anger. The waters of the mere are hot and, like Grendel's blood, poisonous. 62 It would appear that, as Grendel is dying in the mere, the place, conveniently placed at some distance from Heorot, receives his blood that sustained the heat of his anger when he was alive. Grendel's mother's surprise attack on Heorot causes Beowulf to dive into the waters of the mere, another account that renders a body of water another representational site for articulating masculine tensions and anxieties. The mere is imagined in the poem as a space connected to the sea, as the poetic sense of the Old English mere is the sea. 63 The apparent confusion of the fens and the sea in the poem validates Beowulf's accomplishment as a prodigious swimmer and invests his victory with symbolic significance. Most importantly, however, the passage develops a phenomenological masculine perspective on the environment, a symbolic gaze that Beowulf transfers through his gifts, which he brings from the mere to Hrothgar. The account is also significantly inflected with the isomorphic language of emotion that merges Hrothgar's mind and Grendel's mother's mere. When Hrothgar expresses his sorrow caused by AEschere's death, he is on hreon mode (troubled/angry in his mind) (l. 1307b). As Gillian R. Overing remarks, the adjective hreoh may refer to moods and weather, meaning in different contexts "angry" or "stormy". 64 In the Breca episode, the word is applied to the weather's hostility. Hrothgar's mind thus seems to 60 Discenza,Inhabited Spaces,147. 61 In her brief analysis of "The Ruin", in which thermal waters overflow the walls of the ruin, Leslie Locket argues that "for a reader who is aware of the psychological significance of heat and seething in the breast, this constellation of imagesthe seething liquid and the 'bosom' of the buildingsunmistakably evokes emotional distress", Anglo-Saxon Psychologies, 67. 62 See Lockett, Anglo-Saxon Psychologies, especially 59, for the discussion of the use of verbs weallan and (ge)belgan in such contexts. 63 Roberta Frank argues that the Beowulf poet uses the word in a poetic sense to suggest a seascape, while in Old English prose the word refers to fresh water, '"Mere" and "sund"', 154. 64 Overing, "Men in Trouble", 30-31.

metaphorically internalise the violent storm of Grendel's mother's mere and the sea. In his edition of Beowulf, Klaeber glosses hreoh "rough, fierce, savage, troubled" and hreohmod as "troubled in mind, fierce". In his glossary, Klaeber suggests that the word is related to blod-, guð-, wael-reow (2008: 399). In line 548a, Beowulf remembers the turbulent sea-waves that he and Breca had to contend with during their swimming match: hreo waeron yþa (waves were ferocious) (l. 548).

Hrothgar's own account of Grendel's mother merely supplies an image of an environment replete with objects and beings that are elementally transgressive as they transverse such dichotomies as land/water and ground/air. They inhabit a secret land, wolf-slopes and windy promontories, a dangerous passage across the fen, where a mountain-stream under the shadow of cliffs vanishes under the surface of the earth. It is not far from here in miles that the lake stands. Over the lake hang groves covered with frost, wood, whose roots are firm, are overwhelmed by water. There a night wonder is seen, fire in the water. No wise man lives who knows the place. Even when pursued by hounds, a stag, proud with antlers, seeks the wood put to flight, would sooner give up its life on the lake's shore than seek refuge and hide its head in its waters. This is not a pleasant place! Surging water rises to clouds when the wind blows during the inclement weather until they stifle the air and the sky weeps with rain (1357b-1376a).

The horrific place is a forest, but also a fen whose waters resemble sea-waves. The use of the word mere is also evocative of the sea, even though both Grendel and his mother are said to inhabit the fens and moors, and the word fyrgenstream is defined by the Toronto Old English Dictionary as "mountain-stream". The place is also characterised by inclement weather. Its waters are stormy and transgressive, termed yðgeblond (a mingling of waves) (l. 1373a) to suggest that they rise to the sky. His description might therefore seem a projection of his mental disturbance. In The Heat of Beowulf, Daniel C. Remein provides a detailed analysis of what he terms the deforming style that invigorates the passage. He reconsiders the compound yðgeblond in terms of the Old English poetic hydraulic model of the mind. As he points out, the verb astigan, used in the passage above to indicate that the movement of the disturbed waters is upwards, often appears in cardiocentric descriptions of swollen minds. 65 Hrothgar's stormy anger and the reflection of Grendel's now subdued pride in the mere's surging water are thus mediated in the poem through phenomenological poetics of landscape, conflating mental states and physical locations.

Through such projections, the account of Beowulf's fight with Grendel's mother inspires an intense ecophobia. 66 Her abode is transformed, as its turbulent waters will eventually subside as Beowulf emerges victorious from them. The forest surrounding the mere and the mere's waters, for example, is imagined by Hrothgar as inhospitable. The phrase wudu wyrtum faest (wood, whose roots are firm) (l.1364a) can sound menacing when Beowulf's sea-voyage is remembered. The wood from which Beowulf's ship is made is described as a flexible material, wudu bundenne (bound wood, a ship with a bound prow) (l. 216b). Here, around the mere, the wood is, however, faest and unlikely to be capable of serving as construction material. Hrothgar's closing remark may, however, assuage to some extent the anxiety that this monstrous space inspires. He promises to reward Beowulf with wundnum golde (l. 1382a). The wound gold is a human-made object that symbolises human triumph over earth as a natural element: metal is made into an object by human hands, an object that is elaborate and intricate in shape. Beowulf's victory over Grendel's mother will thus not only satisfy Hrothgar's loss. It is also supposed to bear a representative force that will revise Hrothgar's perception of the hostile landscape that is not far from Heorot (l. 1361b-1362). Since the description is addressed to Beowulf, it sounds as if Beowulf was being teased and challenged by Hrothgar's description. His sea-crossing to the Danes (l. 207b-228) appeared to be an optimistic account of a successful territorialisation of the sea as a potentially hostile space.

In addition, the waters of the mere appear to be gendered, but their gendering suggests an imbalance that must be redressed on masculine terms. As Heide Estes and Nicole Guenther Discenza demonstrate, there is a strong connection between the waters of the mere and Grendel's mother's femininity. Both Heide Estes and Discenza describe the mere as a womb-like space. As Discenza puts it, "the infant Beowulf once emerged from a watery feminine space; now Beowulf must struggle in a second such space to survive and re-emerge". 67 She also observes that Grendel's Mother's hostility defies the expectations of a mother's nurturing role. 68 Estes bases her reading of the monstrous landscape in Beowulf on Julia Kristeva's investigation into the symbolism of menstrual blood, the mere being associated with menstruation and blood and represented as "a 65 Remein, "The Heat of Beowulf, 162-3. "In instances in which the words wylm or weallan serve to mark disturbing or even disease-like affects within the Old English corpus, they often occur along the verb astigan (to go up, mount up, risein medical contexts, to spread), as here, "yðgeblond up astigeð' (wave-tossing mounts upwards)" 163. In light of Francis Leneghan's analysis of righteous anger in Beowulf, it might be said that if Beowulf's vengeful action represents divine justice, then his actions against Grendel's mother might be conceived of as punishing the violent inhabitants of the mere, "Beowulf, the Wrath of God", 394-5. For an authoritative account of the Old English poetic concepts of cardiocentric heat and the boiling mind, on which Remein's analysis rests, see Lockett, Anglo-Saxon Psychologies, especially 59-62. 66 Remein's argument, regarding the use of nominal compounds in Hrothgar's description of the mere, emphasises the deformation of human discourse towards more-than-human poetics. He claims that in the description "under pressure, the aesthetic heat of these compound words is deforming and pressurising of both the poem and the human sensorium", The Heat of Beowulf, 163. My argument is, however, aligned with an ecocritical and ecofeminist hermeneutics of suspicion to uncover the anthropocentric gender imbalance in these representations. 67 Discenza,Inabited Spaces,149. 68 Ibid.

kind of vagina dentata, monstrously fearsome in its geographical formulation, the toothy animals reaching out to grab Beowulf as he descends". 69 Beowulf's men are represented as killing Grendel's mother's other offspring. The beasts other than the Grendelkin inhabiting the mere are allegorical, ceasing to exist as Grendel's mother dies. 70 "Beowulf re-inscribes the space in which Grendel and his mother lived: once womb/ dwelling, it is now a tomb". 71 Estes argues that Beowulf's emergence from the mere is "a re-birth from a feminized, liminal marsh, neither land nor sea, into a masculine social order". 72 Beowulf dives into Grendel's Mother's mere in full armour, complete with a helmet and a sword. 73 It has often been argued that the poet's insistence on Beowulf's use of weapons in his combat with Grendel's mother reflects his larger design of representing each subsequent combat as more difficult than the earlier. 74 The number of military objects that Beowulf brings suggests that Grendel's mother is more dangerous than Grendel. Also, Beowulf defeats Grendel in one round. It will take two rounds of fighting to defeat his mother. The fight with Grendel's mother is, yet, not as dangerous as Beowulf's fight with the dragon in the second part. Beowulf dives into the mere as an assemblage of a human body and cultural objects metaphorically raised to the social status of his retainers. Beowulf braced himself, donning armour. He did not fear for his life. His shirt of mail, woven with hands, broad and elaborately decorated, was determined to explore the 69 Estes, Anglo-Saxon Literary Landscapes, 47. 70 Ibid., 48. 71 Ibid. 72 Ibid. 73 The subject presupposed in these passages is weapnodman, a compound which in Old English designates a man. The male child could be referred to with such compounds as waepnedbearn or waepnedcild. A comprehensive discussion of Old English vocabulary pertaining to male and female life-courses can be found in Izdebska, "Weapon-Boys and Once-Maidens". The word makes one appearance in Beowulf in the narrator's comparison of women's strength to that of 'waepnedmen /þonne heoru bunden, hamere geþruen, /sweord swate fah swin ofer helme /ecgum dyhtig andweard scireð' [armoured men, when a bound sword, forged with hammers, stained with sweat, cuts asunder a boar decorating a helmet] (1284b-1287). The comparison is made to compare Grendel's mother's strength to the prowess of men she is to oppose at Heorot. While in prose and much of the Old English verse the word tends to function as a dead metaphor referring to men in general rather specifically to armoured warriors, in this passage from Beowulf the narrator seems to evoke the word's etymological meaning, its referent being exclusively fully armoured men. Fred C. Robinson explains that when the word appears in lines 1284-1287 the use of apposition results in "[exercising] in the audience's mind in noticing elemental meanings or words or in recalling etymological origins", "Two Aspects of Variation", 66. The passage may also be a form of prolepsis, as Beowulf will eventually approach Grendel's mother in his her mere in full armour. 74 Orchard, Pride and Prodigies, 28-29.

waters of the lake. It knew how to protect the bone-chamber of his body, so that a hostile grasp could not harm his heart and life, a malicious hold laid on him by the angry one. Indeed, the glittering helmet protected the head. The helmet stirred up the bottom of the lake and sought the commotion of water, decorated with treasure, enveloped with splendid bands that a long time ago a weapon smith wrought with wondrous skill, beset with ornamental boars, so that no swords could bite him (Beowulf, lines 1441b-1454).

Beowulf's body, bancofa (a bone-enclosure) (l. 1445a), is enclosed and protected by his military accoutrements. At first, the fragment presents Beowulf as a subject and things as objects or tools. He readies himself by donning his military gear. In addition, the narrator reports that his mail-shirt was made by a human hand (or, rather, a man's hand). However, the imagery that develops in these lines goes on to undercut this initial perception that Beowulf is the agent of his actions, a perception that might imply that the weapons he is carrying are mere extensions of his body and act at his will. As Gillian R. Overing points out, military objects function as "the entire warrior apparatus that comprises armour, [and] are also integrally connected to bodies, and to the process of masculine embodiment in particular". 75 Overing observes that as each element comprising Beowulf's military gear "displays a life and will of its own", it also seems "imbued with the capacity to think independently about its function, and even possesses a degree of interiority". 76 As James Paz points out, in this fragment, "Beowulf draws each aspect of his fortitude from the external things he is putting on". 77 The Beowulf poet thus again suspends human agency at a critical moment to highlight the agency of objects. The locus of experience and action is distributed among human and nonhuman bodies.

Overing and Paz's new materialist readings demonstrate that the representation of Beowulf's body and his weapons as one flesh or an assemblage is a moment of inclusion that creates an intimacy between the human and nonhuman. 78 Admittedly, it is the armour that figuratively perceives the hostile environment. Beowulf's armoured body's investigative and penetrative agency produces a representation of the mere as an object of anthropocentric and androcentric perception and action. Beowulf's morethan-human body is not unlike that of a cyborg. In their provocative reading of the episode, Asa Simon Mittman and Patricia MacCormack claim that adorning the warrior with military equipment creates a new being:

[the weapons] are both worn and wielded, carried and utilized as the subject of the warrior, and so they create an intimacy with the body akin to fleshly extension and thus subject modification, creating an emergent new warrior through bodily adornment. 79 However, when reconsidered not from a new materialist, but from an ecofeminist perspective, the passage does not present a new being independent of human, and pervasively androcentric values. On the contrary, the complex assemblage of Beowulf's body and objects becomes an apparatus that operates upon nature as an object. More precisely, 75 Overing, "Beowulf, A Poem in our Time", 323. 76 Ibid., 325. 77 Paz, "Beowulf as Weyland's Work", 81. 78 Dana Oswald utilised the concept of assemblage, predicated upon Deleuze and Guattari's concept of becoming, to discuss the combat between Beowulf and Grendel, Monsters, Gender, Sexuality, 87-88. She evokes the example of the knight and the rider that Cohen develops to elucidate the assemblage formed by Beowulf and Grendel's bodies, Monsters, 87. See Cohen discussion of the knight and his horse an assemblage, Medieval Identity Machines, xxiii. 79 Mittman and MacCormack, "Rebuilding the Fabulated Bodies", 358.

the environment of the mere is transformed into an object of masculine penetration. The emergent assemblage of the human self and material objects is a rhetorical device that perpetuates masculine heroic values, as well as informing the poem's masculinist exploration narrative.

As a pre-modern text, Beowulf offers a pre-Cartesian understanding of an embodied and materially embedded subject. However, as an epic poem imbued with an ambition to represent the materialist values of its readers' class and rank, it also inculcates their ideological perception of landscape and the environment. The poem presupposes a dominant masculine perspective that embeds Beowulf in a manmade apparatus. From an eco-critical point of view, the representation of Beowulf's body and manmade objects is a symbolic site of exclusion. As both Beowulf and objects appear as subjects within this material assemblage, the passage presents a phenomenology of an armed and armoured body. The account renders the environment an object of masculine gaze and violation, mediating the environment through an androcentric representation. The mere becomes an object of masculine perception and representation, in addition to being a setting for masculine action and agency.

The narrative also supplies a man-centred revision of Grendel's Mother. Of course, Grendel's mother is not entirely denied subjectivity in the poem. On the contrary, when she is first described in the poem, she is depicted as a grieving mother who seeks revenge for her son's death. 80 She is also described as a noble woman, ides aglaecwif (noble female adversary)(l. 1259) and queen owning a hall. 81 This dignified characterisation of Grendel's mother is reinforced by the statement of her ownership of the place. As Fabienne Michelet puts it, "to occupy, to delimit, to defend [land]: this is precisely what is at stake in Beowulf", and this triad of functions is mostly ascribed to kings in Beowulf. Secondly, her actions can be identified as masculine, in that her attempts to penetrate Beowulf's body also construct a masculine gender for her, or at least blur a boundary between male heroic behaviour and the queen's role of a host, who peacefully receives her guests of honour in the hall. 82 Indeed, Grendel's mother might be said to be represented as king rather than queen in the poem. Notably, the narrator misgenders her with a masculine pronoun (se, instead of seo, is used at line 1497b), partly eliding Grendel's mother's femininity. M. Wendy Hennequin studies the overlap between the use of heoldan in Beowulf and the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle to argue that "Grendel's mother is not constructed along the lines of queens, but of kings". 83 The account of Beowulf's fight with Grendel's mother juxtaposes his weaponised body with her body that becomes weaponised through its monstrous transspecies features. Most importantly, Grendel's mother's body is resilient to the Beowulfian embodied 80 Jane Chance argues that Grendel's mother represents an inversion of the heroic ideal of ides, a noble woman, especially a monstrous inversion of Wealhtheow, who represents an ideal of peace-weaver, The Woman as Hero, 97. 81 As Kathryn Hume claims, Grendel and his mother's dwelling has many features of a typical hall. "It is a hof, niðsele, hofsele, reced; it has a flet, treasure and walls hung with weapons. In harmony with this style of dwelling, Grendel's mother is an ides as well as an aglaecwif", "The Concept of the Hall", 68. The vocabulary used in Beowulf to refer to her rule over the mere parallels the vocabulary referencing kingship in Anglo-Saxon Chronicle. See Hennequin, "We've Created a Monster", for this argument 82 Dana Oswald reads Beowulf and Grendel's mother combat as sexualised, with Grendel's mother on top of Beowulf, Monsters, Gender, and Sexuality, 95. Mary Dockray-Miller argues that Modthryth and Grendel's Mother construct a masculine identity for themselves, Motherhood, 88-89. On the masculinisation of Grendel's mother see Dockray-Miller, Motherhood and Mothering in Anglo-Saxon England, 95; Oswald, Monsters, Gender, and Sexuality, 95-96. 83 Hennequin, "We've Created a Monaster", 511.

and militarily equipped apparatus both literally and symbolically. As Megan Cavell puts it, Grendel's and Grendel's mother's bodies incorporate embodied weapons. Cavell, in her seminal discussion of monstrous bodies in Beowulf, claims that both Grendel and Grendel's Mother instil horror as hybrids that "blur the line between body and object' and that "embody the precious objects of armour and weaponry". 84 As Cavell observes, what makes these bodies monstrous is precisely their uncanny resemblance to and simultaneous difference from the human body … Because constructed objects like armour are symbols of civilisation, their monstrous embodiment sets the monsters apart from both the world of culture and nature. 85 Hrunting, Unferth's sword that Beowulf holds, cannot penetrate her and fails. While Beowulf's cumbersome war-gear provides ample protection against the inhabitants of the mere, Grendel's mother attacks (gescod, 1502b) him with atolan clommum (terrifying claws) (l. 1502a) and laþan fingrum (hostile fingers) (l. 1505b). Despite her efforts, she does not succeed in wounding and disabling Beowulf's halan lice (unhurt body) (l. 1503a).

While Beowulf's body is equipped with metal objects to resist penetration and dismemberment, Grendel's mother's body is thus weaponised in the sense that her skin and bones have the resilience of manmade armour. The hero perceived the mighty sea-woman, the female creature of the deep. He gave a powerful thrust with his battle-sword, his arm did not withhold strength. On her head the sword sang a greedy heroic lay. The guest found that the shining sword would not bite and harm her life. The sword failed the lord in need; it had suffered through many combats, cutting through the helmets and armour of those destined to die. It was the first time that the glory of the dear treasure had waned (ll. 1518-1528)

The sword hitting Grendel's mother's head resounds, as it were, with a voracious warsong, and yet it cannot bite through her skin and skull. The metaphorical song creates for a moment a single phenomenal field for Beowulf and Grendel's mother, who share the same aural experience. In fact, her agency and subjectivity become the dominant force in this phenomenological horizon, as she is able to overpower and neutralise Beowulf's gaze and senses. His sword becomes weak and useless, and Beowulf casts it away. The personified sword is described as having weakened (geswac, l. 1524b). During her combat with Beowulf, Grendel's mother appears dangerous because she appears to 84 Cavell, "Constructing the Monstrous Body," 158. 85 Ibid., 163.

refuse to be subsumed into Beowulf's masculine phenomenal field as an object. In fact, the text creates a phenomenological horizon for Grendel's mother, from which she returns the Beowulfian gaze and strikes back. In the phenomenal field of the mere, Grendel's mother is a subject and agent too, and, increasingly, her cognition, embodiment and physical violence become a dominating presence, making a strong impression on Beowulf's senses and embodied experience. Benjamin A. Salzman has recently explored the significance of song in Beowulf for its ambiguous potential to create networks of both intimacy and exclusion 86 (2020: 32-34). In his discussion of the poem, he mostly focuses on Grendel. While Grendel's is excluded from the aural community of Heorot, Grendel's mother's body is able to interact with the sword Hrunting, a personified representative of Heorot's community, 87 and create a song that overpowers both the sword and Beowulf's arm. Eventually, Beowulf defeats Grendel's mother by means of a giant sword, whose hilt carries an inscribed account of the biblical flood. Beowulf not only defeats her but also neutralises her by having Grendel's mother disappear from the ensuing narrative. 88 Instead of presenting Grendel's mother's body that posed a serious threat to him, Beowulf brings from the mere Grendel's head, which he severed from his body with the sword that he also used to overcome Grendel's mother. He thus objectifies the body of Grendel and commodifies it into a gift presented to the Danes. Grendel's mother's body, however, never materialises as an object to look at and interpret. Dana Oswald, in her discussion of monstrous bodies in Beowulf, claims that in his account of the fight with Grendel's mother delivered later to Hygelac, Beowulf replaces Grendel's mother's body with that of her son, leading to her erasure from the narrative of the combat with the Grendelkin, 89 In addition, both Grendel's body and his mother's body are dehumanised, to use Rosi Braidotti's formulation, "the sexualised, racialised, and naturalised others, who reduced to the less than human status of disposable bodies". 90 Grendel's mother's subjectivity is thus revised and eventually excised from the poem's malestream perspective. Grendel's mother's identity is overshadowed by the narrator's investment in her role as Beowulf's worthy opponent to be finally excised from the narrative. Her body exists in the narrative insofar as it serves the interests of the masculine embodiment.

In addition, Grendel's mother becomes a resource for masculine exploitation both literally and symbolically. While most of the treasure that lies in the hall is not plundered by Beowulf, his act of plundering includes the carrying away of Grendel's head and the hilt of the giant sword, two trophies which he identifies as saelac (sea-booty) (l. 1652a). These gifts shape the perception of space that Beowulf invaded and plundered, contributing to the role Grendel's mother's mere presence plays in the ideological representation of the landscape that the Danes will hold on to.

86 Salzman, "Community, Joy, and Intimacy", 32-34. 87 In lines 1455-64, the sword is personified as a friend and companion. 88 Oswald, Monsters, Gender, Sexuality, especially 107-9. 89 Monsters, Gender, Sexuality, 106. 90 Braidotti, The Posthuman, 15. It is notable that the Beowulf poet remarks that the Danes do not care for Grendel's life when Grendel escapes from Heorot to die in the mere: "ne his lif-dagas leoda aengum /nytte tealde" [no one among the nation cared for is life and counted his days as meaningful] (793-794a). The phrasing seems to be underscored by a notion that Grendel is attributed some subhuman status by the Danes.

div.head: Beowulf's Sea-Booty
div.p: Before he comes back from the mere with gifts, however, an interesting moment generating much suspense over the outcome of Beowulf's expedition takes place when the Danes give up their hope that Beowulf might emerge alive from the mere and return to Heorot. The Geats, however, remain and wait. Is it a moment of relief for Hrothgar and the Danes? Would not the Geats take over the burden of responsibility for avenging Beowulf, a feat they would not accomplish and become as helpless as the Danish heroes are now, themselves unable to avenge AEscere's death? Beowulf's climactic emergence from the waters is a moment that invests him with political agency and a measure of symbolic power over Hrothgar and the Danes. If representations of triumphs over nature are conceived of in terms of currency in the exchange of honour that assigns status and validates rank among men, Beowulf will be the dispenser of pleasure that can be derived from his account of Grendel's mother's defeat and his successful probing of the depths of the mere. Beowulf's representational powers derived from his objectification of hostile landscapes, sea depths, and monstrous lakes generate anxiety in the Danes and in Hrothgar. Beowulf's success posits him as the major agent in the environment projected as a masculine discursive formation. This anxiety is wisely resolved by Beowulf, who bestows the hilt on Hrothgar as sae-lac (sea-booty) (l. 1652a), an important gesture that confirms Hrothgar's reputation as the greatest king be saem tweonum (between the two seas) (l. 1685b). Beowulf's own words and the poet's description of his gesture stabilise the symbolism of the sea as a referent for Hrothgar's rule. While Hrothgar cannot validate his power in battle, Beowulf's gesture constructs the king as a masculine subject who exercises authority through gaze. The scene counterbalances the earlier moment of Grendel's mother's sensual and aural domination within the mere.
Hwaet! we þe þas saelac, sunu Healfdenes, leod Scyldinga, lustum brohton tires to tacne, þe þu her to locast
Listen! We have brought you sea-booty, the son of Healfdene, man of the Scyldings, with joy as a sign of glory, so that you can look at it (ll. 1652-1654).

Beowulf expressly identifies the sea as the source of the gift, aligning the mere with the sea rather than the fens. The gift is also transferred to Hrothgar, becoming the token of his rule. The exclamatory Hwaet (listen!) (l. 1652) directs the assembled women and men's attention to the gifts and Hrothgar's as their recipient. The centre of the phenomenal field becomes, however, ultimately, located in Hrothgar's gaze. Hrothgar's gaze embodies the perspective through which the story of the hilt is mediated. While readers of Beowulf questioned the extent to which the Danish pagan ruler can understand the biblical account of the flood, the early medieval English Christian audience participates in the gaze, aligning that which the king actually sees with the narratorial Christian account of the flood as a destruction of monstrous races. Hrothgar spoke, looking at the hild, an ancient inheritance, on which the origin of an ancient strife was described/engraved. The flood killed a tribe of giants, the surging ocean. They suffered terribly. That tribe was strange to the eternal God. The lord gave them retribution by means of the surging of water (ll. 1687-1693).

The biblical flood, referred to as flod (flood) (l. 1689b) and waeteres wylm (seething of water) (l. 1693), becomes in the account the instrument of divine retribution, a token of divine power. God's control of landscape and the environment emerges as a symbol of ultimate power. The simplex wylm, a word appearing in compounds that denote emotions of sorrow and helplessness but also anger, might be an index of God's retributive wrath visited on sinful giants. 91 Hrothgar's gaze validates the malestream view of the mere from a Christian and patristic perspective, a perspective that is supplied by the authoritative voice of the Christian narrator.

div.head: Conclusions: Beowulf's Armed and Armoured Phenomenology of Space
div.p: Recent discussions of the human and nonhuman world in Old English literature emphasise the intimacy between both. 92 This article by no means discourages readers of Beowulf from exploring the poem as ecopoetry. What this paper argues is that representations of the isomorphism of the human and non-human world are not free from textual violence and anthropocentric ideology. I would conclude that the analysis presented in this article comports well with Alfred K. Siewers's argument that representations of landscape in Beowulf (and Guthlac A) reflect the Western acquisitiveness for the environment as a standing reserve, replete with resources both for consumption and an instrument for exercising power and domination. 93 Men in Beowulf, I have argued here, adduce representations of the environment to create and resolve crises of masculine identity, power and domination. The narrator also uses ambivalent imagery of welling and surging to represent men in power. Both the physical environment and its representation are symbolic sites for the construction of authority and domination in the poem.

div.head: Disclosure Statement
div.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

div.head: ORCID
div.p: Jacek Olesiejko http://orcid.org/0000-0002-7047-2498

91 For an argument that Beowulf's anger is "the manifestation of the wrath of God" aroused by Grendel's evil see Leneghan, "Beowulf, The Wrath of God", especially 392. 92 See especially Dale, The Natural World in the Exeter Book Riddles, especially 20-25; Soper, Life-Course, especially 12-14, where she interrogates the concepts of anthropomorphism and isomorphism. 93 Siewers, Landscapes of Conversion.

note.p: On Grendel as an inversion of human and heroic norms see Dubois, "The Unity of Beowulf".ENGLISH STUDIES https://doi.org/10.1080/0013838X.2025.2585317

Estes, 37. Estes points out the ecocritical position on such depictions obscure the practices of exploitation of the sea, although it has to be admitted that Anglo-Saxons did not exert as negative an impact on the sea as our contemporaries, Anglo-Saxon Literary Landscapes, 38.

As Price observes, "Beowulf's masculinity depends not only upon demon strations of his masculine prowess but also upon revealing the inferiority of other men -whether Breca or Unferð", "Sufficient Tragedy". 687.
